name: Little Langford
coordsSource: web-verified:os-grid:SU047366(GENUKI)+gazetteer.org.uk+wikidata:Q11761979; postcode-corrected SP4->SP3; no listings to cross-check; 2026-06-04
overview: Little Langford is a hamlet and former civil parish, now part of Steeple Langford parish, lying on the south side of the River Wylye in the Wylye valley, roughly 5 miles north-west of Wilton and about 10 miles from Salisbury. It is essentially Little Langford Farm together with the Grade II*-listed Norman church of St Nicholas of Mira, whose carved south doorway and tympanum date to around 1120. The former parish had a population of just 64 in 1931 and was merged into Steeple Langford in 1932; much of the land has long been part of the Wilton estate of the Earl of Pembroke.
character: About as remote and rural as Wiltshire gets - an isolated farm and ancient church set among riverside meadows and downland, with effectively no village around them.
amenities: No shop, pub or services within the hamlet
Nearest everyday shops and pubs in the neighbouring Langford/Wishford villages
Main shopping in Wilton and Salisbury

schools.name: Wylye Valley CE Primary School
schools.distance: ~5 mi up the valley at Codford (the nearer Steeple Langford site closed 2010)

commutes.notes: Via the A36 which runs along the Wylye valley
From Salisbury station; the Warminster-Salisbury rail line passes through the valley but has no stop here

prices.summary: Very few open-market transactions: the hamlet is dominated by farmland (much historically owned by the Wilton estate) and a handful of houses, so sales are rare. Use the wider SP3 Wylye-valley sold-price record for context.

thingsToDo: Visit the Norman Church of St Nicholas with its c.1120 carved tympanum
Walking and birdwatching along the River Wylye and its flood meadows
Explore the wider Wylye valley and nearby Grovely Wood

placesToEat.name: Royal Oak, Great Wishford
placesToEat.notes: ~1.5 mi south at Great Wishford; 17th-century ivy-clad village pub, restored and reopened

pros: Notable historic church on the doorstep
Easy A36 access to Salisbury and Warminster
cons: No amenities, shop, pub or school within the hamlet
Extremely limited housing stock - sales are rare
whoItSuits: Buyers wanting maximum rural seclusion in the Wylye valley and willing to drive for all amenities.

sources.label: Church of St Nicholas, Little Langford - Britain Express

Ofsted - Wylye Valley CE Primary School, Codford
